text: Lift upper lobectomy is initiated by dividing the mediastinal pleura over the interior aspect of the superior vein.
In this instance, dissection is being continued superiorly.
A regional and mediastinal lymphadenectomy has been performed.
Now working between the superior vein and the inferior vein, the regional lymph nodes should be removed.
It cannot be overemphasized the value in this maneuver in both dissection as well as oncologic care.
Once these lymph nodes are removed, the superior vein and bronchial plane can be developed.
The vein can be grabbed with the vascular forceps and retracted and the dissection plane between the bronchus and vein is developed bluntly.
Dissection is continued in this area and then superiorly between the pulmonary artery and the vein.
Once the plane is sufficiently dissected, then the blunt forceps can be used to get around the vein and develop the plane behind it to pass the endovascular stapler.
Once again, if the forceps can be opened completely behind the vessel, there is sufficient room to pass endovascular stapler.
Again, if a monofilament tie
is felt to be helpful to retract the vein.
This is quite feasible.
Once the vein is divided, the regional lymph nodes around the anterior and apical branch of the pulmonary artery should be removed.
This will facilitate isolation of the pulmonary artery, a critical step in the left upper lobectomy.
Once the artery is divided, again, it could be stapled from the posterior incision through use of an endovascular stapler.
Once the anterior apical branch of the pulmonary artery has been divided, attention should be paid towards the left upper lobronchus.
It is critical to remove the peribronchial lymph nodes, particularly in the interlobar area.
This will facilitate isolation of the bronchus and passage of the endovascular stapler to divide it.
It is very important to divide that first branch of the pulmonary artery in order to facilitate passage of the stapler to divide the bronchus.
Leaving that artery branch intact will result in too much tension on the artery and risk for vascular injury.
stapler can be passed through the posterior aspect of the incisions.
Once again, the instrument arm needs to be moved temporarily and the bronchus is then divided.
Once the left upper lobe bronchus is divided, the remaining branches of the pulmonary artery, including the lingular branch and posterior branches,
can be easily isolated with retraction of the lung laterally.
Here one of the posterior branches is being divided with the endovascular stapler with retraction of the lung with robotic instrumentation.
And lastly, once all the artery branches are divided, the only remaining structure is the major fissure.

The interlobar lymph nodes have been removed by retracting the lung inferiorly and the fissure now can be divided by passing the endovascular stapler from the posterior incision and staying right on the delineated fissure.
It is important to identify and preserve the superior segmental artery.
Stapling of the fissure
can be done last and therefore does not require separate dissection of the pulmonary artery in the fissure unless necessitated by anatomic abnormality or the presence of regional disease.
As in other lobectomy procedures, the now detached lobe can be placed in the
scopic bag and brought out through the utility incision.
